title: Bart Has Two Mommies

jokes.text: Why does god always need money? It's a lot of little stuff. God has to pay all the elves in his workshop. Plus he's got all those planets to support. Did you see that ring he gave saturn?
jokes.setup: Lisa questions why God needs money at church fundraisers
jokes.punchline: Bart's childish logic treating God like Santa with elves, plus the Saturn ring quip
jokes.explanation: Classic Simpsons blend of childish logic with cosmic concepts. The Saturn ring line is particularly clever wordplay on engagement rings.

jokes.text: Who knew saints had such fat heads? It's all a big scam. This booth? No, religion in general.
jokes.setup: Homer comments on carnival game prizes
jokes.punchline: Escalates from carnival booth scam to condemning all religion
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer character moment - his casual dismissal of religion while focused on carnival games. Great misdirection structure.

jokes.text: Building a taller steeple than the one on that snooty episcopal church across the street. Reverend, why do we really need this? To compensate for my own sense of smallness.
jokes.setup: Church fundraiser ostensibly for God's work
jokes.punchline: Lovejoy's brutally honest admission about his personal insecurities
jokes.explanation: Excellent character work showing Lovejoy's unexpected self-awareness and honesty about his psychological motivations.

jokes.text: That's the new femac, the computer designed just for women. The 'you've got mail' voice is Susan Sarandon. You've got mail, unlike the prisoners at Guantanamo Bay. Get informed.
jokes.setup: Description of women's computer with celebrity voice
jokes.punchline: Jarring shift to political commentary about prisoner rights
jokes.explanation: Bold political humor that's unexpected from Lovejoy. The tonal whiplash creates the comedy but feels slightly forced.

jokes.text: How can you be sure? 'Cause he wants it.
jokes.setup: Marge asks how Homer can guarantee winning the computer
jokes.punchline: Homer's simple, childish logic about Bart wanting it
jokes.explanation: Pure Homer logic - confusing desire with ability. Perfect economy and character voice.

jokes.text: Someone's not on their mark. That's better. Go!
jokes.setup: Duck race about to start
jokes.punchline: Pedantic attention to exact positioning for rubber ducks
jokes.explanation: Mild absurdist humor about taking a silly race too seriously. Functional but not memorable.

jokes.text: Protect the duck. D'oh! Protect the duck. Uh! PRoduck the tect.
jokes.setup: Homer trying to help his duck while getting battered
jokes.punchline: His speech becoming garbled as he's hit repeatedly
jokes.explanation: Physical comedy gold with Homer's signature 'D'oh!' The scrambled speech shows good escalation structure.

jokes.text: Hey! This race is for rubber ducks, not meat ducks.
jokes.setup: A real duck has somehow joined the rubber duck race
jokes.punchline: Official's matter-of-fact distinction between 'meat ducks' and rubber ones
jokes.explanation: 'Meat ducks' is a wonderfully absurd phrase. The deadpan bureaucratic tone makes it funnier.

jokes.text: A living duck? I wished too hard and he became real.
jokes.setup: Real duck wins the race
jokes.punchline: Homer's magical thinking explanation
jokes.explanation: Homer's childlike magical thinking perfectly in character. The Pinocchio reference adds layers.

jokes.text: This is my favorite kind of surprise: Mild.
jokes.setup: Ned wins the computer unexpectedly
jokes.punchline: His enthusiastic celebration of mildness
jokes.explanation: Perfect Ned Flanders line - enthusiastic about being boring. Great character integration and economy. Highly quotable.

jokes.text: The fact that you tried means everything. Hey, that's the same thing you said to Bart after he choked in that soccer game. And we both know he sucked. And how. But this is different.
jokes.setup: Marge consoling Homer with generic encouragement
jokes.punchline: Homer pointing out her pattern of hollow consolation, then doubling down
jokes.explanation: Great escalation structure and family dynamic humor. Homer's awareness of the pattern but continuation anyway.

jokes.text: You know, all those complete strangers googling each other makes my flesh crawl.
jokes.setup: Ned doesn't want the computer
jokes.punchline: His innocent misunderstanding of what 'googling' means
jokes.explanation: Ned's sheltered perspective on technology. The sexual innuendo he doesn't realize he's making is perfectly in character.

jokes.text: Well, sir, we could do a little quid pro for the kaypro. That left-handed convention is in town and as the owner of Springfield's largest southpaw shop, I just got to be there. Could you nanny-goat my kid-diddly-ids?
jokes.setup: Ned wants to trade the computer for babysitting
jokes.punchline: His tortured wordplay combining 'quid pro quo' with computer brand, plus 'nanny-goat'
jokes.explanation: Peak Ned Flanders wordplay. 'Nanny-goat' and 'kid-diddly-ids' are wonderfully absurd while staying in character.

jokes.text: Our last baby-sitter let them watch comedy central. Now Rod makes me check the closet every night for Rita Rudner. No Rudner.
jokes.setup: Explaining why he needs a new babysitter
jokes.punchline: Rita Rudner as a boogeyman figure, plus his ritual confirmation
jokes.explanation: Brilliant absurdist concept - Rita Rudner as a monster children fear. The 'No Rudner' confirmation is perfect deadpan follow-through. Highly quotable and original.

jokes.text: Okay, game over. It's a tie. That means we both come in second. We're number two!
jokes.setup: Sitting-still contest ends in a tie
jokes.punchline: Flanders boys' innocent enthusiasm about being 'number two'
jokes.explanation: Mild scatological humor enhanced by the boys' innocent delivery. The logic error about ties is also funny.

jokes.text: Wow, it's the big unit himself, Randy Johnson. You fanned more men than Salome. Yeah, I get that a lot.
jokes.setup: Ned meeting baseball pitcher Randy Johnson
jokes.punchline: Biblical reference comparing strikeouts to Salome's seduction, Randy's weary response
jokes.explanation: Clever biblical reference from Ned mixing sports and scripture. Randy's deadpan response suggests he's heard every possible compliment.

jokes.text: You have one for a mailman? A bear can't be a mailman. Now how many Dr. Bears do you want? They come in boxes of a thousand. One box. You and me got a problem.
jokes.setup: Ned asking about different profession bears
jokes.punchline: Randy's arbitrary rules about bear professions, then aggressive sales tactics
jokes.explanation: Absurd logic about what jobs bears can have, then the intimidating sales approach. Good escalation from silly to threatening.

jokes.text: I'm a chimpmunk. I eat nuts without saying grace. I'm a permission slip.
jokes.setup: Boys playing dress-up
jokes.punchline: Rod's religiously guilt-ridden chipmunk, Todd's mundane costume choice
jokes.explanation: Perfect Flanders children humor - Rod's religious anxiety even in play, Todd's bizarre literalism. 'I'm a permission slip' is wonderfully absurd.

jokes.text: And could you ask mommy to come to daddy in a dream and tell him how to cut our sandwiches? And please tell Mrs. Simpson it's rude to eavesdrop on our prayers. But we forgive her.
jokes.setup: Boys praying at bedtime
jokes.punchline: Practical request about sandwich cutting, then breaking fourth wall about eavesdropping
jokes.explanation: Excellent multi-layered joke: mundane sandwich concerns in prayer, plus meta-awareness of being overheard. Great character work and structure.

jokes.text: Ready? Ready. And joust! That was awesome! Let's do it again. A canoe made of country ham? You know I'll eat it.
jokes.setup: Homer and kids playing dangerous games
jokes.punchline: Their enthusiasm for violence, plus Homer's food obsession overriding safety
jokes.explanation: Typical Homer parenting - encouraging dangerous behavior. The country ham canoe is a nice absurd touch showing his food priorities.

jokes.text: What's wrong with your teeter-totter? Daddy says up and down see-saws are dangerous. Chocks?! Why don't we try it... freestyle.
jokes.setup: Flanders boys' over-cautious playground equipment
jokes.punchline: Bart's incredulous reaction to safety chocks, then suggesting 'freestyle' danger
jokes.explanation: Good contrast between Flanders over-protection and Bart's recklessness. 'Freestyle' seesaw is a funny concept.

jokes.text: El mundo es loco!
jokes.setup: Flanders boy experiencing wild playground action
jokes.punchline: Sheltered child's dramatic Spanish exclamation about the crazy world
jokes.explanation: Perfect reaction from a sheltered Flanders child experiencing normal playground chaos. The Spanish adds unexpected flair.

jokes.text: So, Marge, in tomorrow's game, I'm gonna recover a fumble just for you. Maybe you'll run it in for a touchdown. Don't pressure me. I get enough of that from my dad.
jokes.setup: Homer acting like a high school athlete impressing his girlfriend
jokes.punchline: His commitment to the delusion including parental pressure
jokes.explanation: Homer's complete immersion in teenage fantasy is perfectly in character. The dad pressure detail shows commitment to the bit.

jokes.text: Dad, mom's over at the Flanders', baby-sitting nimRod and nimTodd.
jokes.setup: Bart reporting where Marge is
jokes.punchline: Clever name-calling combining 'nimrod' with Rod and Todd
jokes.explanation: Brilliant wordplay perfectly fitting Bart's character. Economical and highly quotable insult.

jokes.text: I want to go to the showbiz animal retirement home. They take in animals from the movies and tv that aren't cute anymore.
jokes.setup: Lisa wants to visit somewhere
jokes.punchline: Brutal honesty about entertainment industry discarding aging animal actors
jokes.explanation: Brilliant dark concept about Hollywood's treatment of animal actors. Lisa's matter-of-fact delivery makes it funnier.

jokes.text: Do you think any of the gremlins from the gremlins movie are there? Sure. I am gonna get them so wet.
jokes.setup: Bart asking about movie creatures at the retirement home
jokes.punchline: His plan to trigger their transformation, showing he misses the point entirely
jokes.explanation: Perfect Bart response - completely missing the pathos and focusing on chaos. Good character integration with his destructive nature.

jokes.text: Sad? You're crazy. I feel like I'm at the oscars for the animals I forgot existed. Aflac!
jokes.setup: Homer reacting to the retirement home
jokes.punchline: His positive spin comparing it to awards show, plus recognizing the duck
jokes.explanation: Homer's ability to find joy in sad situations. The Aflac recognition shows his pop culture awareness.

jokes.text: I'm not a real bear! That was just the name of my character. Now let me out! Hey, you know where the bathroom is? Maybe I do and maybe I don't.
jokes.setup: Yogi Bear protesting his imprisonment
jokes.punchline: Breaking fourth wall about being fictional, Homer's unhelpful response
jokes.explanation: Meta-humor about fictional characters acknowledging their fictional status. Homer's coy bathroom response is perfectly unhelpful.

jokes.text: Word on the street is: Cover the toilet seat with tissue. Sweet... sweet tissue.
jokes.setup: Prison-like conditions at retirement home
jokes.punchline: Treating toilet paper as precious contraband, sensual delivery
jokes.explanation: Great prison movie parody with toilet paper as currency. The sensual delivery makes it memorable.

jokes.text: One of them played the out-of-control judiciary in a republican party commercial.
jokes.setup: Describing toot-toot's children's careers
jokes.punchline: Monkey cast as metaphor for judicial system in political ad
jokes.explanation: Sharp political satire about using animals to represent abstract concepts in attack ads. Very timely and clever.

jokes.text: No, no, it's still 'kid-napped.' The prefix applies to the victim.
jokes.setup: Someone says 'ape-napped' about Bart
jokes.punchline: Pedantic correction about proper kidnapping terminology
jokes.explanation: Perfect Comic Book Guy moment - prioritizing pedantic accuracy over emergency. Great character integration and linguistic joke.

jokes.text: A chimp that size could tear your son's head right off. But she'll leave the rest of him alone?
jokes.setup: Warning about chimp's dangerous strength
jokes.punchline: Homer's focus on the practical upside of selective violence
jokes.explanation: Homer's bizarrely optimistic take on his son being partially decapitated. Dark humor with perfect character logic.

jokes.text: Call me ned zeppelin, but is one of my boys abrased and contused? I cut myself on the knife, playing christian clue. The secular humanist did it in the schoolhouse with misinformation!
jokes.setup: Ned finding evidence of injury
jokes.punchline: 'Ned Zeppelin' pun, then Christian version of Clue with ideological villains
jokes.explanation: 'Ned Zeppelin' is brilliant wordplay. Christian Clue with 'secular humanist' as the villain is perfect Flanders satire. Excellent craft and highly quotable.

jokes.text: I cut myself on the knife, playing christian clue. The secular humanist did it in the schoolhouse with misinformation!
jokes.setup: Child explaining injury
jokes.punchline: Christian version of Clue with ideological villain
jokes.explanation: Brilliant parody perfectly suited to Flanders family. 'Secular humanist with misinformation' is inspired.

jokes.text: They're all I've got since that sad day when maude found eternal happiness.
jokes.setup: Ned explaining his overprotectiveness
jokes.punchline: Euphemistic way of describing his wife's death
jokes.explanation: Beautifully written line showing Ned's faith-based coping mechanism. 'Found eternal happiness' as euphemism for death is poetic and funny.

jokes.text: Well, their mascot is a kneepad wearing a helmet.
jokes.setup: Describing the safety-focused play center
jokes.punchline: Mascot that embodies extreme safety consciousness
jokes.explanation: Great visual gag concept - safety equipment as a character. Perfectly captures over-cautious parenting culture.

jokes.text: You haven't seen Bart for a few hours, so you automatically assume I let something terrible happen. I didn't say that. I know what you think: When stupid Homer wasn't looking, Bart got kidnapped by a monkey.
jokes.setup: Homer being defensive about losing track of Bart
jokes.punchline: His sarcastic scenario that turns out to be exactly what happened
jokes.explanation: Brilliant dramatic irony - Homer's sarcastic exaggeration is literally true. Great structure and character work.

jokes.text: Help me, Lisa! I have serious mental problems!
jokes.setup: Homer's defensive rant continues uncontrollably
jokes.punchline: Sudden self-aware cry for help
jokes.explanation: Homer's rare moment of self-awareness about his psychological issues. Good escalation from his rambling.

jokes.text: Sorry, lady. I've never eaten fresh fruit and I reckon I never will.
jokes.setup: Lisa offering fruit to the monkey
jokes.punchline: Monkey's stubborn refusal with country accent
jokes.explanation: Funny subversion - monkey rejecting natural food. The country accent adds personality.

jokes.text: This oreo is rank.
jokes.setup: Bart eating with the monkey
jokes.punchline: Complaining about stale cookies while kidnapped
jokes.explanation: Bart's priorities - food quality matters even in crisis. Shows his adaptability to weird situations.

jokes.text: Then i'll come back and feed the monkey a peach full of antifreeze.
jokes.setup: Lisa planning to rescue Bart
jokes.punchline: Innocent Lisa casually planning to murder the monkey
jokes.explanation: Shocking dark humor from sweet Lisa. The casual, matter-of-fact delivery makes it funnier and more disturbing.

jokes.text: I'm not Ned. I'm a... friendly dog. I'm not a good liar.
jokes.setup: Ned trying to spy without being detected
jokes.punchline: Terrible lie followed by immediate confession of lying inability
jokes.explanation: Perfect Ned character moment - his Christian honesty makes him terrible at deception. Great self-awareness.

jokes.text: He chipped a tooth! How will he bite wheat thins? How?!
jokes.setup: Rod gets injured at the play center
jokes.punchline: Ned's specific, mundane concern about snack crackers
jokes.explanation: Hilariously specific concern showing Ned's anxious parenting. Wheat Thins as the primary worry is perfectly absurd.

jokes.text: Local boy Bart simpson is being held hostage by an angry chimpanzee who has improved both his hygiene and posture.
jokes.setup: News report about hostage situation
jokes.punchline: Noting positive aspects of chimp care
jokes.explanation: Good news parody finding silver lining in crisis. Economical and well-structured.

jokes.text: His so-called real mother could not be found for comment.
jokes.setup: News report about Bart's kidnapping
jokes.punchline: Implying Marge might not be Bart's real mother due to the situation
jokes.explanation: Media sensationalism joke. The 'so-called' adds unnecessary drama to a straightforward situation.

jokes.text: Well, we're gonna do what every kid your age likes to do: Look at bread. No! The 'n' word!
jokes.setup: Ned planning activities with his sons
jokes.punchline: Bread-looking as fun activity, boys' horror at saying 'no'
jokes.explanation: Brilliant multi-layered joke: boring bread activity, plus 'n-word' misdirection to 'no.' Perfect Flanders family dynamic. Excellent craft and quotability.

jokes.text: Uno?! That's a gateway game to pinochle.
jokes.setup: Boys want to play card games like with Marge
jokes.punchline: Treating Uno like gateway drug to more serious card games
jokes.explanation: Brilliant gateway drug parody applied to innocent card games. Perfect Ned paranoia and excellent economy. Highly quotable.

jokes.text: She made us feel happy. And not church-happy. For real happy.
jokes.setup: Boys explaining what they miss about Marge
jokes.punchline: Distinguishing between forced religious joy and genuine happiness
jokes.explanation: Profound insight from children about authentic vs. performed happiness. Great character development and social commentary.

jokes.text: Lisa, monkeys don't have feelings. If they did, then my experiments could be called cruel.
jokes.setup: Lisa suggesting the monkey has emotions
jokes.punchline: Frink's circular logic protecting his conscience
jokes.explanation: Dark satire of scientific rationalization. Frink's self-serving logic about animal consciousness is brilliantly written.

jokes.text: Get me a lemur, or a marmot. Or Tom Greene, he's not doing anything these days. And really stick it to him on the money.
jokes.setup: Krusty needs a new sidekick
jokes.punchline: Comparing Tom Green to animals, noting his career decline
jokes.explanation: Typical Krusty cynicism about celebrity worth. The Tom Green reference was timely. Good escalation structure.

jokes.text: Okay, but don't let our hands touch. It's gay. What's 'gay' mean? It means you used to be afraid, but now you're not.
jokes.setup: Bart and Rod escaping together
jokes.punchline: Bart's homophobic comment, then beautiful redefinition of 'gay'
jokes.explanation: Brilliant subversion turning casual homophobia into beautiful definition of courage/happiness. Sophisticated writing that works on multiple levels.

jokes.text: Okay, but don't let our hands touch. It's gay. What's 'gay' mean? It means you used to be afraid, but now you're not.
jokes.setup: Boys climbing down together
jokes.punchline: Innocent redefinition of 'gay' as overcoming fear
jokes.explanation: Clever handling of sensitive topic through innocent misunderstanding. Good structure and character work.

jokes.text: I'm gay, daddy! I'm gay! Mrs. Simpson made me gay! I believe he's saying he's okay.
jokes.setup: Rod using Bart's definition of 'gay'
jokes.punchline: Ned's panicked misunderstanding, then rational explanation
jokes.explanation: Perfect callback payoff with great misdirection. Ned's panic followed by understanding creates excellent structure. Highly quotable.

jokes.text: I haven't seen climbing like that since Dudley Moore married Susan Anton! Bob, why did I wait a hundred years to get you up here? You're tellin' me. Now I can look down Dolly Parton's dress whenever I want.
jokes.setup: Muppet characters commenting on the action
jokes.punchline: Height difference marriage joke, then perverted motivation for being in balcony
jokes.explanation: Standard Muppet Show style humor. Dated celebrity reference and mild sexual humor. Feels like filler content.

tmdb_overview: The family is at a church fund-raiser, where Homer tries to ensure his rubber duck onto victory, but Flanders wins the big prize, which he doesn’t want and gives it to Marge. In return Marge agrees to watch Rod and Todd while Ned is attending a left-handed convention. While Marge is spending all her time with the Rod and Todd and trying to let them learn how to be boys; Homer takes Bart and Lisa to a home for former celebrity animals and a monkey kidnaps Bart. Marge’s ability to be a mother comes into question when Ned discovers what she is doing with his boys and the local news covers Bart’s current predicament.
